sections.heading: What is a REST API?
sections.content: A REST (Representational State Transfer) API is a standardized architectural style for building web services that communicate over HTTP. It defines a set of rules for how clients (like Android apps) request and receive data from servers. REST APIs are the backbone of modern mobile app networking — they power everything from user authentication and fetching feeds to submitting forms and syncing data. In Android, your app acts as the client, sending HTTP requests to a server and receiving responses — typically formatted as JSON — that you parse and display in your UI.

sections.heading: Core REST Principles
items: Client-Server — the client (Android app) and server are separate; the server manages data, the client handles UI
Stateless — each HTTP request is independent; the server does not store client session state between requests
Uniform Interface — resources are identified by URLs (endpoints); interactions happen through standard HTTP methods
Resource-based — everything is a resource (User, Product, Order) identified by a unique URI
Representation — resources are transferred as a representation, most commonly JSON or XML
Layered System — the client doesn't know if it's talking directly to the server or through intermediaries (proxies, CDNs)
Cacheable — responses can indicate whether they are cacheable, improving performance for repeated requests
JSON is the standard — virtually all modern REST APIs use JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) for data exchange

rows.purpose: Retrieve a resource or list of resources from the server
rows.example: GET /users — fetch all users; GET /users/42 — fetch user with ID 42

rows.purpose: Create a new resource on the server
rows.body: JSON body with new resource data
rows.example: POST /users — create a new user with body {"name": "Alice"}

rows.purpose: Replace an entire existing resource with new data
Partially update an existing resource — only changed fields
rows.body: Partial JSON body with only updated fields
rows.example: PATCH /users/42 — update only the email field of user 42

rows.purpose: Remove a resource from the server
rows.example: DELETE /users/42 — permanently delete user with ID 42

rows.purpose: Same as GET but returns only headers, not the response body
rows.example: HEAD /users/42 — check if user exists without downloading the full response

sections.heading: HTTP Request Anatomy
sections.content: Every HTTP request sent from your Android app to a server has the same structure: a method (GET, POST, etc.), a URL (the endpoint), headers (metadata about the request), and optionally a body (data sent with POST/PUT/PATCH requests). Understanding each part is essential for correctly configuring your Retrofit calls and debugging network issues.

sections.heading: HTTP Request Components
items: Method — the HTTP verb: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE
URL — the full endpoint: https://api.example.com/v1/users/42
Base URL — the common prefix: https://api.example.com/v1/
Path — the resource path after base URL: /users/42
Query Parameters — key-value pairs after '?': /users?page=1&limit=20&sort=name
Headers — metadata key-value pairs: Content-Type, Authorization, Accept, User-Agent
Body — the JSON payload for POST/PUT/PATCH requests: {"name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com"}
Content-Type header — tells the server the format of the body: application/json
Accept header — tells the server the format the client expects in response: application/json
Authorization header — carries authentication credentials: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...

rows.meaning: Request succeeded — response body contains the requested data
Resource created successfully — typically returned after a POST request
Request succeeded but no response body — common for DELETE operations
The request was malformed — invalid JSON, missing required fields, or bad parameters
Authentication required or failed — missing or invalid token/credentials
Authenticated but not authorized — user does not have permission for this resource
The requested resource does not exist on the server
Request conflicts with current state — e.g., creating a duplicate email account
Request is well-formed but semantically invalid — validation errors on submitted data
Rate limit exceeded — client is sending too many requests; must slow down
Unexpected server-side error — not the client's fault; retry with backoff
Server temporarily unavailable — maintenance or overload; retry later

sections.heading: JSON — The Language of REST APIs
sections.content: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, human-readable data format used by virtually all modern REST APIs. It represents data as key-value pairs (objects) and ordered lists (arrays). In Android, you parse JSON responses into Kotlin data classes using libraries like Gson, Moshi, or kotlinx.serialization. Understanding JSON structure is fundamental to correctly defining your data model classes and configuring your JSON parser.

sections.heading: JSON Structure Basics
items: Object — curly braces containing key-value pairs: {"id": 1, "name": "Alice", "active": true}
Array — square brackets containing ordered values: [{"id": 1}, {"id": 2}]
String values are in double quotes: "name": "Alice"
Number values are unquoted: "age": 25, "score": 98.5
Boolean values: "active": true, "deleted": false
Null values: "middleName": null
Nested objects: "address": {"city": "Delhi", "country": "India"}
Array of objects: "orders": [{"id": 1, "total": 99.99}, {"id": 2, "total": 49.50}]
The root of a JSON response can be either an object { } or an array [ ]

sections.heading: JSON to Kotlin Data Class Mapping

rows.kotlin: data class User(val id: Int, val name: String, val email: String)
data class Status(@SerializedName("is_active") val isActive: Boolean, @SerializedName("created_at") val createdAt: String)
data class AuthResponse(val user: User, val token: String)
Root is List<Item> — Retrofit returns List<Item> directly for array responses
data class PaginatedResponse<T>(val data: List<T>, val total: Int, val page: Int)

sections.heading: Common API Patterns
sections.content: Most REST APIs follow predictable patterns for structuring their endpoints, handling authentication, and formatting responses. Understanding these patterns helps you quickly integrate any new API into your Android app and anticipate how to handle responses, errors, and pagination.

sections.heading: RESTful URL Patterns
items: Collection endpoint: GET /users — returns a list of all users
Single resource: GET /users/{id} — returns one user by ID
Create: POST /users — creates a new user with body data
Update (full): PUT /users/{id} — replaces the user completely
Update (partial): PATCH /users/{id} — updates only specified fields
Delete: DELETE /users/{id} — removes the user
Nested resources: GET /users/{id}/orders — returns orders belonging to a specific user
Filtering: GET /products?category=electronics&minPrice=100
Sorting: GET /users?sort=name&order=asc
Pagination: GET /users?page=2&limit=20 or GET /users?cursor=abc123

rows.how_it_works: A static key passed as a header or query parameter
rows.use_case: Public APIs, weather services, map APIs

rows.how_it_works: A signed token obtained after login, passed in Authorization header
rows.use_case: Most modern apps — login returns a JWT used for subsequent requests

rows.how_it_works: Base64-encoded username:password passed in Authorization header
rows.use_case: Simple internal APIs; not recommended for production mobile apps

rows.how_it_works: Token-based authorization flow; app gets access token from auth server
rows.use_case: Sign in with Google, Facebook, GitHub — delegated authorization

rows.how_it_works: Short-lived access token + long-lived refresh token; refresh token gets new access token
rows.use_case: Keeping users logged in without requiring re-authentication

sections.heading: Common HTTP Headers in Android Networking
items: Content-Type: application/json — tells the server the request body is JSON; required for POST/PUT/PATCH
Accept: application/json — tells the server to return JSON; usually default for REST APIs
Authorization: Bearer {token} — carries the auth token; added to all authenticated requests
User-Agent: MyApp/1.0 Android/14 — identifies your app to the server for analytics and debugging
Cache-Control: no-cache — instructs the server not to serve a cached response
Accept-Language: en-US — requests localized content in the specified language
X-Request-ID: uuid — unique ID per request; useful for distributed tracing and debugging
In Retrofit: add persistent headers via OkHttp Interceptor so they are sent automatically on every request

rows.response: Returns items for that page number
rows.pros_cons: Simple to implement; items can shift between pages if data changes

rows.response: Returns items starting from the offset
rows.pros_cons: Flexible; same shifting problem as page-based when items are inserted

rows.response: Returns items after the cursor; response includes next cursor
rows.pros_cons: Stable — no item shifting; more complex; cannot jump to arbitrary pages
Very performant with database indexes; requires stable sort key

sections.heading: Error Handling Strategies for API Calls
items: Check HTTP status code — any 2xx means success; 4xx is a client error; 5xx is a server error
Parse error response body — APIs often return JSON error details: {"error": "email_taken", "message": "Email already in use"}
Handle network errors — IOException means no connectivity or timeout; not an HTTP error
Handle HttpException — Retrofit throws HttpException for non-2xx responses when using suspend functions
Use try-catch in Repository: try { val response = api.getUsers() } catch (e: HttpException) { } catch (e: IOException) { }
Use Result<T> wrapper to propagate success and error states cleanly to the ViewModel
Implement exponential backoff for 5xx errors — wait 1s, then 2s, then 4s before retrying
Never show raw technical error messages to the user — map error codes to user-friendly strings

rows.cause: No internet connectivity, DNS failure, connection timeout, server unreachable
rows.handling: Show offline message; retry with connectivity check

rows.cause: Server took too long to respond — exceeds the configured timeout
rows.handling: Show retry option; increase timeout or check server health

rows.cause: Client error — bad request, unauthorized, not found, validation failed
rows.handling: Parse error body for details; show appropriate user message; 401 → redirect to login

rows.cause: Server error — internal error, service unavailable
rows.handling: Show generic error message; implement retry with exponential backoff

rows.cause: SSL certificate validation failed — invalid, expired, or self-signed cert
rows.handling: Never disable SSL validation in production; fix the server certificate

rows.cause: API returned malformed JSON or unexpected schema
rows.handling: Log the raw response for debugging; show generic error to user

sections.heading: Testing and Debugging REST APIs
items: Use Postman or Insomnia to test API endpoints manually before writing Android code
Check the API documentation — look for base URL, endpoint paths, required headers, and request/response schemas
Use Android Studio's Network Inspector or OkHttp logging interceptor to inspect actual requests and responses
Add OkHttp's HttpLoggingInterceptor to log full request/response bodies in debug builds
Use MockWebServer (from OkHttp) to mock API responses in unit and integration tests
Inspect the raw JSON response before writing Kotlin data classes — use jsonformatter.org or Postman
Enable strict mode in Gson/Moshi to catch unknown JSON fields early in development
Test error scenarios explicitly — disable network, return 401, return 500 — don't only test the happy path

sections.heading: REST API Best Practices for Android

rows.practice: Always perform network calls on a background thread
rows.reason: Network on main thread throws NetworkOnMainThreadException and freezes the UI
Clean, structured concurrency with automatic thread switching via OkHttp dispatcher
Enables offline access and reduces unnecessary network requests

rows.practice: Never hardcode API keys or tokens in source code
rows.reason: Keys in source code are exposed in version control and decompiled APKs

rows.practice: Use HTTPS for all API communication
rows.reason: HTTP is insecure — all data is transmitted in plaintext and can be intercepted

rows.practice: Implement request timeout and retry logic
rows.reason: Mobile networks are unreliable — without timeouts, requests can hang indefinitely
Only handling 200 OK leaves your app broken for common scenarios like 401, 404, or 503

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: REST APIs and HTTP form the communication backbone of virtually every Android app that connects to the internet. Understanding HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), status codes, headers, JSON structure, authentication patterns, and error handling gives you the foundation to confidently integrate any web service into your app. In the next topics, you will learn how Retrofit and OkHttp implement these HTTP concepts in Android with a clean, type-safe, coroutine-friendly API that removes all the low-level networking boilerplate.
